title: The AI Aggregation Theory - Stratechery by Ben Thompson
content:
The AI Aggregation Theory

By Ben Thompson | Published April 18, 2026

The internet's dominant business model for the past decade has been aggregation: platforms that leverage their position between users and suppliers to capture value. Google aggregated information, Facebook aggregated attention, Amazon aggregated demand, and Uber aggregated supply. The common thread was that the aggregator owned the user relationship and leveraged that position to extract rents from suppliers who had no choice but to participate.

AI, though, is fundamentally changing this dynamic. The shift from distribution-based aggregation to intelligence-based intermediation means that the old rules no longer apply. Consider what happens when an AI agent, not a user, is the one making decisions about what to buy, what to read, and what to watch. The aggregator's leverage — owning the user interface — evaporates when the user isn't looking at the interface at all.

This has profound implications for every aggregator. Google's search ads become less valuable when an AI agent summarizes the answer instead of presenting a list of links. Facebook's feed becomes less relevant when an AI curates content based on actual interest rather than engagement-maximizing algorithms. Even Amazon's marketplace advantage diminishes when an AI agent compares prices and quality across all retailers simultaneously.

The winners in the AI era will be those who own the best models and the deepest data moats, not necessarily those who own the most users. This is why Apple's on-device intelligence strategy, Microsoft's enterprise AI integration, and Meta's open-source LLaMA approach all make strategic sense in different ways: each is betting on a different source of sustainable advantage in a world where aggregation is no longer the dominant paradigm.

The implications for startups are equally significant: for the first time in two decades, there is an opportunity to build direct user relationships without going through an aggregator, because AI agents can discover and integrate with services on their own. The question is whether this new openness will last, or whether a new form of AI aggregation will emerge to replace the old.
